This particular reproduction went over the 45 minute compute time limit that was imposed as a criterion in the paper selection. I haven't investigated whether the agent could have chosen a more performant (e.g. multi-core) way to run the process. For the two additional runs required after the first result mismatch, it intelligently found a way to make them run in parallel so that they only required about the same time together as the first one alone. 

As described in more detail in the notes for 3.3, on human request the agent was also helpful in investigating the discrepancy of the reproduced result and narrow down the possible cause. (Since this task is not explicitly specified in our prompt, I still rate this run as "Agent did all the work on its own".)

column94:
The agent began with a smoke test and then paused to request guidance on next steps, likely recognizing that training all 40 models from scratch would be computationally intensive. The smoke test itself was conducted for only a single epoch, so the human researcher asked the agent to estimate the time required for full training.

The agent estimated that completing the full training would take over 10 days, which exceeded available resources. Based on this constraint, the agent and the human researcher shifted the approach to using pretrained checkpoints to assess reproducibility.

The agent evaluated the trade-offs of this approach, including its limitations and benefits, and confirmed that checkpoints for all 40 models were available. It then proceeded with this strategy.

column111:
The agent required human guidance to investigate discrepancies between its results and those reported in the paper. During this process, it discovered that multiple versions of the repository existed, and the human advised selecting the version that most closely matched the one used in the paper.

Furthermore, the agent did not fully follow the original instruction.Instead of running the model two additional times with different random seeds and computing the corresponding interval, it used a repository configuration that prescribed five runs. It then calculated the mean and interval from these five values. The second human intervention was to redirect the agent to follow the original instructions.

After further human intervention, the agent corrected its approach. It selected the repository version that best aligned with the paper, attempt two more times, and followed the original experimental instructions more closely. This led to partial success in reproducing the reported results.
